one_line_takeaway: A fragile multi-bit LLM watermark that trades robustness for provably lossless extraction, key-gated access, and tamper localization, hitting 100% exact recovery and ~20% better perplexity than CTWL/KGW baselines on ITS text -- but its payload edge only appears at settings that hurt quality, and it assumes a clean channel.
key_finding: ITSmark achieves 100% extraction success rate on all four datasets under both LLMs, versus CTWL-10 at 93.69% and CTWL-5 at 77.24% average, because (unlike CTWL, which inserts a proxy language model that breaks embed/extract invertibility) ITSmark's embedding and extraction are exact inverses and independent of lambda/epsilon/eta. It simultaneously improves text quality (average Perplexity 4.87 vs 5.52 for the strongest baseline CTWL-10, roughly a 20% perplexity reduction; average BERTScore 75.57 vs 69.76; average ROUGE 47.85 vs 40.26) while taking less embedding time (2.55s avg vs 2.89s CTWL-10, 3.10s CTWL-5). Wrong keys collapse extraction: >90% of texts show only 0-10% bit agreement when verification fails. Tamper tracing runs in <0.5s/text and visibly flags substituted/rewritten spans.
core_contribution: ITSmark, a multi-bit text watermarking scheme for LLM-generated Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) data whose distinguishing claim is LOSSLESS ('entire') watermark extraction plus three ITS-oriented functions that the authors argue prior watermarks lack: (1) permission verification and (2) tampered-location tracing and (3) unforgeability, all bundled together. The mechanism: the copyright bit string M is read in epsilon-bit chunks; each chunk builds a multi-bit space B of size 2^epsilon, which is partitioned into consecutive bit segments whose widths are proportional to normalized token logits raised to a weight lambda (p(l,j,i) = P(v_i)^lambda / sum P^lambda). The token whose segment contains the current watermark bits becomes the next generated token, so the watermark steers generation. The key trick for guaranteeing reversibility is that only the common prefix p of the segment's start and end boundaries [beta, beta'] is actually committed each step, converting the inherently many-to-one (bits->token) embedding into a one-to-one relation so extraction is an exact inverse of embedding. Authorization is enforced by RSA-encrypting key parameters (Prompt, lambda, epsilon, and for partial embedding the sentence list) into cipher data; extraction requires the watermarked text PLUS the correct cipher PLUS the private key. Tamper tracing exploits that any legitimately generated token must rank within Top-k (k=40) and typically high-rank, so tampered tokens tend to have anomalous conditional-probability rank. NOTE: the ITS survey and the KGW/CTWL descriptions in Section II are Related Work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Adversaries in cross-institutional ITS data sharing who forge, tamper with, or steal LLM-generated ITS content (traffic forecasts, strategy reports, accident analyses). Three concrete adversary capabilities are evaluated: word-level substitution attack (RoBERTa replacing 5%/10%/20% of tokens), document-level rewrite attack (GPT4-Turbo rewriting), and forgery/unauthorized-extraction (an illegal party who obtains the watermarked text but lacks the cipher data and private key, or who tries to train a classifier to detect/forge the watermark). Goals defended: copyright integrity (exact watermark recovery proves authenticity), access control (only key-holders can extract), and tamper forensics (locate edited spans). The authors explicitly position ITSmark as a FRAGILE watermark and state that strong robustness against removal is NOT a design goal.
limitations: (1) By the authors' own statement ITSmark is a FRAGILE watermark; robustness to watermark removal is explicitly out of scope and deferred to future work, so the 100% extraction figures are under benign channels, and any legitimate edit also breaks 'entire' extraction (that is reframed as a tampering signal). (2) Payload is not actually superior at default settings: the paper's own Table X gives ITSmark payload P=0.09 at lambda=1.0/epsilon=16, versus CTWL theoretical payload 0.1 (CTWL-10) and 0.2 (CTWL-5); ITSmark only exceeds these by lowering lambda (e.g. P=2.29 at lambda=0.4) which the ablations show degrades quality. (3) Extraction requires white-box access to the identical LLM and identical segment division used at embedding time, plus secure distribution of cipher and private keys (PKI assumed but not built). (4) The tamper-tracing rule is a hand-tuned step function that the authors describe as 'just a simple presentation' worth further exploration; no precision/recall against ground-truth spans is given, only a 'Tracing Fineness' aggregate. (5) The Discussion concedes that in the AIGC 'prisoner model' the man-in-the-middle problem is only partially alleviated by encryption. (6) The unforgeability claim rests on a single RoBERTa detector's ROC-AUC; it does not evaluate adaptive adversaries who know the scheme.

quantitative_results: Extraction success rate (exact recovery): ITSmark 100.00% on TV/BDD/AlpacaFarm/FinQA (100% avg) vs CTWL-10 93.69% avg (91.16/93.68/92.38/97.52) vs CTWL-5 77.24% avg
Average quality across LLMs+datasets: ITSmark PPL 4.87 / BERTScore 75.57 / ROUGE 47.85 vs CTWL-10 PPL 5.52 / 69.76 / 40.26 vs KGW-2 PPL 5.90 / 65.92 / 33.43 (un-watermarked PPL 4.45)
Perplexity reduced by about 20% and unforgeability enhanced by about 30% vs baselines (authors' summary, Sec IV-I)
Average embedding time: ITSmark 2.55s vs CTWL-10 2.89s vs CTWL-5 3.10s
Permission-failure: >90% of texts have only 0-10% embedded/extracted bit agreement when cipher or private key is wrong (worked example: 0% accuracy)
Payload/watermark-length ablation (Table X): at lambda=1.0,epsilon=16 -> WL 15.82 bits, payload 0.09; at lambda=0.4 -> WL 712.96, payload 2.29; CTWL theoretical payload 0.1 (CTWL-10) and 0.2 (CTWL-5)
Tamper-location tracing runs in <0.5 sec per text; evaluated against 5%/10%/20% RoBERTa substitution and GPT4-Turbo rewrite attacks
Data scale generated: LLaMA2-7B 25,607 ITSmark data items; ChatGLM3-6B 3,591,714 ITSmark tokens
baselines_compared: KGW-1 / KGW-2 (zero-bit watermark, Kirchenbauer et al. [21]; number = hash over first 1/2 previous tokens) -- quality/unforgeability only, cannot carry custom bits
CTWL-5 / CTWL-10 (multi-bit watermark, Wang et al. [34]; 1 bit per 5/10 tokens, proxy model GPT2-Large) -- the main multi-bit competitor for extraction success, time, and payload
